content:
The morning came brilliantly, the shimmers of worry ceasing from Vornakir after the night prior. He woke early, earlier than the rest of his dorm-mates for a purpose. He stepped into the kitchen and took a heavy breath; He hadn't done anything like this since the night he prepared desserts for Redd for his date. And he sensed a hint of tension between the others, but aside from that his previous sessions in counselling had helped bolster himself and carving away some of the shames he'd faced in the past weeks. He stood prideful there in the kitchen and with a flourish of his hand, a spark of ochre arcana flared and formed itself into an ethereal hand. It moved with a life of its own, by his will as if it were attached to him and he'd never lost his left arm. He reach back to pull his hair back into a tight, distinguished ponytail and cracked his neck to the side before he waved his hand and willed the mage hand to gather what was needed. 

It brought back various ingredients and he took what was left and brought it from the cabinets. Extra-virgin olive oil, a pound of sweet sausage, an onion and some ciabatta bread, a jar of tomato sauce with herbs (marinara), some provolone cheese, some Parmesan, fresh basil, five of the largest eggs they had and freshly ground pepper as well as salt. The hand brought forward an apron to drape over him, and he reach back to tie it in place appropriately. A snap of his fingers ignited a magical flame on the stove and he poured the oil in a cast-iron skillet to allow it to begin heating. He sliced down the sausage and tossed in bits at a time, stirring it occasionally until all of the meat was in the pan and fairly browned. While it simmered, he chopped up the onion and scraped the sliced vegetables into the pan until they went golden. 

The ciabatta was diced up into small cubes and scraped into the mixture next to allow it to toast, and the sauce followed with a couple of cups of water until it too began to simmer. He pantomimed turning a dial with his fingers, the heat from his magical flame lowering whilst his mage hand dumped in and began to stir the provolone and parmesan cheese as well as about half of the basil, letting everything get to know one another in the pan. 

He pursed his lips, canting his head to the side as he watched it cook. The thought occurred to him to try something a little different, and he flipped the back of his spoon around to make some shallow wells in the mixture within the pan. His mage hand fluttered closer and cracked an egg into each of those five wells, then he covered the pan and let the dish cook until the eggs whites began to set and brown whilst the yolk was still runny. After about ten minutes, he sprinkled the rest of the parmesan and basil on top, and ground up some pepper to top it all off with a generous pinch of salt. 

At last, he waved off the fire and grasped the skillet with a cloth so he could carry it over to the table - Placing it on a special serving platter so the skillet wouldn't burn the table. He sat out several plates and silverware appropriately, as if this were fine dining and carefully carved out the contents to make perfect portions for all of those who would like to come eat - one for each, but whomever didn't want a portion would mean more for someone else. And then he sat down, taking out his own portion to put on the plate and he sat himself down for breakfast alone.
((A reference image for our breakfast))

Redd padded into the kitchen, hair mussed and clothes rumpled, a long yawn betraying the sleepless night behind him. The weeks of spell dueling still clung under his eyes in dark crescents, but the rich smell drifting through the air coaxed a smile to his face all the same. By the time he leaned against the doorframe, a crooked grin had already tugged its way back onto his mouth.

“Well, that explains why the dorm isn’t on fire. Only you would be up at dawn making something that actually smells edible.”

He crosses his arms, eyes tracking the skillet like it might vanish if he blinked. “Should’ve known. The mastermind behind Zurr’ka’s seduction by tart strikes again. Honestly, half my relationship is owed to your pastries“

With a shake of his head, Redd stepped closer, the smirk tugging at his mouth easing into something warmer as he watched the precise way Vornakir plated portions. “You make this look effortless. Almost makes the rest of us look useless by comparison. Caspian’s running out of swords for me to ruin just trying to cut bread.”

He gestured vaguely at himself, grin sharpening into mock despair before his tone softened again. “And you—one arm down and still turning out feasts. If that’s not talent, I don’t know what is.”

Redd dropped into the nearest chair, the hem of his tank top riding up as he slouched back. He snagged a plate, twirling the fork between his fingers. “Not letting you sit here like some tragic one-man dinner party. I’m no Zašia. But you’ll have to settle for my charming company.”

He dug in with exaggerated seriousness, eyes narrowing like a critic about to write a scathing review. The first bite, though, had his brows shooting up despite himself. “...Hells. You keep this up, and Whimsy’s going to start preferring you over me.”

He put the sausage pieces to the side of his plate for Whimsy, then to prove the point, Redd speared a cube of toasted ciabatta and sausage soaked in sauce and flicked it skyward. Whimsy swooped down in a blur of feathers, snatching the prize cleanly before winging back to the rafters with a smug hoot. Redd only shook his head, muttering under his breath, “Traitor.”

((I forgot Redd was vegetarian, so I had to edit it, haha))

He turned his head upward and met Redd's gaze, then offered an incline of his head as well as brandishing his hand outward to a chair and plate. "You are most welcome to join. I thought it nice to make everyone their own breakfast, you'll have to forgive me though. I had forgotten in the heat of the moment despite you having a taste for meat you don't eat it." He offered in jest with a widening grin, hoping that his joke would land. The mage hand floated closer and laid out a cloth beside Redd's plate and his lips parted in a warm chuckle. "I hardly believe my tarts contribute that much but I accept the compliment regardless." He said with his own easy lilt of his head to the side. 

However Redd's words made him shake his head. His draconic, shimmering golden glass petal-like eyes settled on the food as he dove into his mind to fish out the proper reply. "Everyone always says that. I make it look effortless. I appreciate that, more than a lot of people realize. Nobody quite knows the amount of effort that truly goes into it. In truth, magic and painting are two of the very few things that came naturally to me - And I suppose a natural aptitude for learning. Almost every talent I have is born from hard work and hours of practice. My mother used to tell me often, 'Just because you are destined for greatness doesn't mean you should settle for greatness alone; You want for nothing already. *Need* for nothing.' Sometimes it was lost on me, what she meant but over the years I understood more. My people would bend over backward for my favor and cater to me heels over head if I asked. What good am I if everyone does everything for me? I'm nothing more than a pretty face and a puppet for leadership."

He exhaled a heavy sigh and shook his head as he picked at his own food with a deep consideration in his gaze. "Apologies, I just have much on my shoulders these days so I find I'm unloading randomly like this. I appreciate your company. How are things with you and Zurr'ka anyway? I've always thought him to be interesting and strong, since I met him at the welcome party. If I'm being honest, I almost immediately clocked him as an impressive duelist. Had I been more humble back then, I would have asked him to train me. I wanted to befriend him and subtly learn from him at the very least. But alas, I feared I'd be seen as weak if I debased myself and *asked* for help."

Sol padded into the kitchen, bare feet whispering against the floorboards. The sight of the skillet waiting on the table tugged a look of genuine surprise across his face. He wasn’t dressed in silks or the polished attire he usually carried like armor; just a black tank top and yellow, flannel pants, hair knotted into a messy bun that let a few strands fall free. A pair of golden-rimmed rectangular glasses perched on his nose, catching the lamplight in soft gleams. The look was disarmingly ordinary, yet on him, it carried its own kind of charm.

“Alright,” he said with a low laugh, voice still edged with sleep. “Who bribed you to make all this? Because I was fully prepared to grab some stale bread and call it breakfast.” His grin curved genuine, then softened as his gaze shifted to Vornakir. “It smells incredible. Thank you for doing this.”

Moving to the counter, Sol pulled down a small pot and filled it with milk. The bracer at his wrist stirred, runes shimmering faintly as if waking. With a flick of his fingers, molten threads of gold and bronze poured from the engravings like ink. He sketched a fire sigil in the air, his hand steady but carrying that unconscious little flourish, like a performer who couldn’t help but make the simplest gesture elegant. The symbol hung, glowing, before he flicked it downward with two fingers. It sank into the metal, blooming into steady heat until the milk began to steam.

“Anyone want chai?” he asked, though the way he was already lining up cinnamon, cardamom, and cloves suggested he’d made up his mind. He hummed softly under his breath as he crushed the spices with the flat of a knife, the tune wandering aimless through the air.

Glancing back toward the table, Sol wrinkled his nose with a playful tilt of his mouth. “Cheese and I don’t exactly get along,” he admitted lightly, brushing it off with a flick of his hand. “But if there’s any bread and sausage left, I won’t complain.”

Caspian opened the front door to the dormitory. His clothes were a little sweaty from his morning practice but he had washed up fairly well. A damp towel hung around his neck cooling him off. 

He immediately lifted his nose and sniffed the air like a blood hound. Then a grin broke out across his face.

“I was hoping that smell was our dorm! What’s cooking in here?” 

He sees the prepared dishes and tosses his gear to his echo duplicate to bring to his room. The echo gave Redd a wide berth while near the kitchen with the swords Caspian actually used, The man then beelined straight to the food and took in its full glory.  Sitting by an unclaimed plate he didn’t just inhale the contents but he did eat rapidly savoring each bite. Anything that had been set aside and not eaten by the owl he politely took for himself. 

“Damn Scales you really can cook, you’d give a lot of professionals a run for their money. The sausage was cooked just right!”

Seeing Sol preparing drinks Caspian called out, “Oh Sunni I’ll take one!”

Food giving him a second wind Caspian leaned forward intently ready to listen and chime into any ongoing conversations. 

When a lull presented himself he would look at the others and add himself, 

“It’s not very regal and a poor substitute for a duel but maybe you could combine your cooking and baking with magic, Scales? Then do a presentation for sponsorship day. It’d definitely be popular.”

Redd tipped his fork toward Vornakir, the corner of his mouth quirking. “Appreciate you remembering. Truth is, when you’ve been a lot of these animals, it’s weird eating them. Kind of kills the appetite you know.” He gave a crooked grin. “Not that Whimsy minds. Bird of prey. Circle of life and all of that.”

At the mention of Zurr’ka, Redd’s smirk shifted, losing some of its sharpness. He gave a low chuckle, but it landed more thoughtful than amused. “You know me—I’ve always been good at keeping things shallow. A smile, a clever line, a night that doesn’t mean anything. Never stuck around long enough for it to matter?” He toyed with the fork, spinning it in his fingers before letting it drop against the plate with a soft clink. “But him… I don’t know. He’s steady, yeah, but not just that. He’s strong in ways that don’t need to be loud, and weak in ways he tries to hide but can't from me anymore. He’s gruff at first, sure—but less grumpy once you’re actually around him.” He huffed out a breath, half a laugh at himself, half a way to break the weight of the words. “He's also hot. That helps.”

Leaning back, he stretched one arm over the backrest, tank top riding up slightly as his grin found its edge again. “Anyway, enough about me, what about you lot? As Caspian points out, Sponsorship Day’s coming up fast. You planning to dazzle the courts with your brilliance, or just wing it and pray some lord or lady finds you charming?” His eyes flicked between Sol and Caspian, still playful, but the grin eased at the edges. “And if the wrong people make it harder than it should be… you won’t be dealing with them alone.”

Sol’s shoulders stiffened at the word *sponsorship*. His hand hesitated mid-stir, the spoon clinking once against the pot before he steadied it with a breath. With a small wave, golden and silver magic threads shimmered from his hand, sketching a lazy arc over the spoon handle, as it stirred itself. The scent of spiced chai grew richer, steam curling into the air.

He reached for two porcelain mugs from the shelf, identical save for the faint chips along their rims. With a flick of his fingers, the chai streamed smoothly into both, rich and fragrant, steam curling from the surface. He plucked a few ice cubes from the basin and let them fall into his own mug. The hiss and crackle as they met the hot chai brought the faintest smile to his lips. Sol always preferred iced drinks, no matter the season, a small rebellion of comfort in a world that demanded too much formality from him.

Another wave of his hand lifted Caspian’s steaming cup across the table to him, while Sol carried his own 
over, the porcelain cool against his palm. He dropped into the seat beside Caspian, posture loosening as he leaned back. For a moment, the mask slipped; his expression fell at the mention of Sponsorship Day, shoulders tightening as though under invisible weight.

“Don’t even mention Sponsorship Day,” he muttered, stirring the ice idly with a flick of magic to keep it from melting too fast. “I’ve got nothing to present. And to make matters worse,” his voice faltered, softer, “I just got a letter saying my father is coming.”

The admission hung heavy in the air, the faint clink of ice in his mug enhancing the tension in his fingers. Then, as quickly as it came, he pulled the mask back on with a thin laugh, the corners of his mouth twitching up into something too practiced. “But hey, how much worse could his perception of me get, right?”

Redd had been forking through what was left on his plate, picking around the last stubborn bits of sausage and piling them neatly off to the side for Whimsy. A cube of ciabatta had soaked up enough sauce to bleed orange, and he dragged it through the edge of melted cheese before popping it into his mouth.

That was when Sol’s voice dipped. The practiced smile didn’t land the way it should have, and Redd caught the falter between one heartbeat and the next. He chewed slowly, watching him over the rim of his fork.

“Don’t undersell yourself,” Redd said at last, leaning forward to spear another piece of bread. His tone was casual, crooked grin still in place, but there was weight behind it. “Courts would empty their coffers for a silver tongue like yours. Suggestion, illusions—those are talents most of them only dream about. You’ve got more to offer than stale bread and a forced smile.”

He set the fork down, wiping sauce from his lip with his thumb. His grin softened, not gone but edged with something more certain. “When your father shows up, let him look all he wants. The courts’ll be too busy leaning in the second spells fall from your mouth. Some won’t know whether to clap or just gape, and the ones who actually get it?” He tipped his fork toward Sol, smirk tugging back into place. “They’ll be more taken with the man casting them."

Caspian leaned back in his seat. He reached his arms out to stretch. Nothing quite like a good meal after a good workout. 

As the chai was placed in front of him he quickly grabbed it and sipped despite its scalding temperature. The imbued draconic resistance to flame from Aurum had been allowing him to enjoy his food and drink with no delay for it to cool down. A momentary silent revel as he tasted the delicious beverage. It was broken when he noticed Sol tense beside him.

He leaned over and bumped shoulders with his friend. He gave a reassuring smile. 

“First this is great thanks. Second, Redd’s right. Sponsorship is about impressing others not our parents. Plus if you really need it I know you’ve got a pretty damn impressive trick you could share. But I doubt you’ll need it.”

He turned to Redd and gave his own plans,

“My parents arranged an exhibition duel for Cordelia and I to team up. I figured I’d use it to show off my new trick.”

He summoned the echo for added effect. 

“Maybe I can do a demonstration of its applications beyond combat too. What about yourself Redd?”

Sol cradled his glass of chai, the ice clinking faintly as he stirred it with lazy flicks of gold-and-silver magic. His free hand reached behind him for the sketchbook he kept tucked near the kitchen shelf, pencil already turning between his fingers like second nature.

At first, the lines were nothing more than habit; folds of cloth, the suggestion of a silhouette, just enough to settle the knot in his chest. But the strokes grew deliberate, sharper. He found himself sketching a shirt cut with regal symmetry: one sleeve draped long and formal, the other shoulder left bare, framed by structured lines that suggested authority. Beside it, he shaped a vest layered with intricate embroidery, each stitch imagined in metallic threads, geometric motifs curling like sigils across the fabric.

He filled the margins with practiced shorthand: navy silk base, high collar, silver-thread seams, embroidered cuffs. His pencil lingered on the vest, a dignified yet practical garment that spoke of someone who carried themselves with purpose. Without meaning to, he gave the figure a neat ponytail, tied back with a sleek band, hair drawn smooth and deliberate. Regal, but unpretentious.

It wasn’t until he leaned back to sip from his cup that he realized who he had been sketching for. He had sketched clothing that radiated Vornakir’s personality. The recognition tugged a faint laugh from him, and he leaned into it, letting the pencil wander to add tailored trousers with pressed lines and boots polished to a mirror shine.

Only then did he glance up, catching the tail end of Redd’s encouragement and Caspian’s shoulder-bump reassurance. The corner of his mouth tugged into something softer, a smile that didn’t quite mask the waver in his chest.

“You two make it sound easy,” he said, voice gentler than his usual polish. “Illusions and enchantments, I know how to cast them all, but convincing myself it’s worth showing? That’s a different story.” A laugh slipped out, thin but earnest. “Sponsorship Day’s supposed to be about proving yourself, and right now I feel like I’m sketching in the margins, hoping something sticks.”

He tapped the pencil lightly against the page, leaving the design of Vornakir in full view; the one-sleeved shirt, the embroidered vest, and the careful hairstyle all stared back at him. “But I’ll figure something out. I always do.”

With that, he leaned back into his chair, leaving the journal open on the table, his absentminded design laid bare as he folded himself back into the conversation.

Vornakir hadn’t been paying too much attention the last few moments; He had been too consumed with the food and picking at his own brain to concoct a plan for himself. It was pretty easy to tell whatever was on his mind devoured all thought and attention, however as if his brain shot past a lag spike due to poor ping and caught up with itself he snapped back into reality. He blinked several times, those glass petal-like draconic hues wide and taking in his surroundings. With a firm, tight lip heavy inhale he fixed his eyes on Sol. 

“Well, as pompous and entitled this may sound…I don’t technically *need* Sponsorship Day. I already have a place in a court, specifically a place far higher than a Sponsor could obtain for me unless they could posit me as an Emperor or a King of a more vast territory. In theory, I’ll either have a significant place in court or be dead within the next couple years at most.” He explained with a dismissive flourish of his wrist, sending his glimmering golden mage hand down the hall to unlock and enter his room while he spoke. That noble drawl of his was coming out in full force, but there was a velvet warmth to it as opposed to Diana’s more chilled tone. The longer he fixed Sol with his stare, the more he may be filled with a sense that Vornakir wanted to be genuine despite how often his words came off as self serving. 

“As such, regardless of that I anticipated going above and beyond if nothing else but to set myself above all others. However, if you would like I could pose you a few options to dull the impact of Sponsorship Day’s expectations for you? I have in my room, several lengthy research documents regarding the dealings of Fey that I was independently looking into, and of course several tomes regarding lore of dragons. Another option, though this one I somewhat advise against because of the fallback, is stealing Sorjin’s thesis concept on the culturally significant tree that grows in my homeland - but that one is fairly difficult unless you have inside information.” He continued on, picking at his food once more politely as to not eat with his mouth full.

He wiped his lips with a cloth, and washed down the remnants with a glass of water before he continued. “Or you can go the political route. We could all hand you a somewhat interesting secret about us, nothing too damning, and allow you to present how ‘well you’re able to infiltrate rival courts and charm your way into secrets via charismatic espionage’. You could write a paper detailing the intricacies of sociopolitical dynamics when people of varying levels of nobility are thrust into a situation where they’re on equal footing with equal resources and demonstrate what the result is during an experiment like that is — which is of course a narrative of what we do here at Spark Oath. As for your father? If you wish, I could as you would say “play it up” on your behalf? Paint you with the brush of a lord’s favor so to speak, tell him of how beneficial your friendship has been to me and whatnot. As prude and uncouth it may be for me to boast about, the fact remains that my position is one that I could abuse the title of if at the very least to make you look good and shed a bit of burden off?” He offered, watching the mage hand floating back to the table to deposit a small sample of his research on Fey as well as one of his tomes on dragons at the empty place of the table before it returned obediently to lock his door.

He then glanced down to the doodle and a small smile touched his lips, a break in the mask. He made a gesture toward it, “May I?”

((Fey research is something he’s been doing on the side as noted in various RP threads in his room when visited by Zašia which had been intended on him helping her find her patron but can be salvaged into a school research paper I’m sure. The dragon tome I imagine is very basic information on draconic lineages, but I’m sure our benevolent god could have input if anything he offered is of interest))

Redd pushed his plate away at last, flicking the last chunk of sausage toward the rafters. Whimsy dove for it in a blur of feathers, hooting smugly as she carried it off. With a crooked grin, Redd pulled Sol’s chai closer, swirling the steam under his nose before taking a long sip. “Not bad,” he muttered, tilting the cup in salute. “Better than anything I’d have managed to close out a meal. Guess this makes it official—best breakfast this dorm’s ever seen.”

He glanced at Caspian, echo standing sentinel in the corner. “Twins in a spell duel… now that would be siiiiick. Courts will be buzzing before you’ve even thrown your first strike. Just make sure your echo doesn’t try to steal the spotlight. It's still twins, not triplets.”

When the question came back to him, Redd set the cup down, shoulders rolling like he needed to shake something off. “Me? Thinking an exhibition match with Zurr’ka. His parents have been going on and on about expecting to see a match from him; figured it’d be a good introduction since they haven’t met me yet. He thinks it’s unnecessary, but… I don’t know. Feels like I need to prove I’m strong enough to stand beside him. Maybe more to myself than to them.”

He let that hang for only a moment before his eyes slid toward Sol’s open sketchbook. A smirk tugged at his mouth. “And you say you’ve got nothing to offer, meanwhile you’ve designed half a wardrobe before breakfast. Keep at it and the courts’ll be begging you to sponsor them.”

Finally, his gaze flicked to Vornakir. “And Scales, I appreciate the offer. Hey, maybe one of us should woo you for a place in your court. Still… if it comes to the secrets angle, I can probably supplement with one or two of my own. You learn a lot when people think you’re not listening—or when they’re too distracted to keep their mouths shut.” His grin sharpened, equal parts mischief and promise.


Redd leaned back again, arm slung over the backrest, grin crooked but easier now. “Whatever we all end up doing, at least we’re not walking into Sponsorship Day alone.”

Sol’s fingers lingered against the edge of his sketchbook before he finally slid it across the table to Vornakir. He didn’t hand it over lightly; the journal was as personal as a diary, pages filled with pieces of himself he rarely showed. “Here,” he said with a faint, wry smile, “it’s more doodles and daydreams than anything useful.”

He glanced at the stack of tomes and papers Vornakir had summoned, the Fey research and dragon lore sliding neatly into place. His lips pressed thin, gratitude and overwhelm pulling in equal measure. “I appreciate this. Really. But that,” he gestured at the pile of research, “that’s you. It’s regal, disciplined, political. All the things my father would want to see from me. But me?” He tapped the corner of the journal Vornakir now held. “I’ve never been that. I’m… creative. Messy. Half my work comes in sketches on napkins or the margins of letters I forget to send.”

At the mention of his father, Sol shifted in his seat, shoulders tightening. “As for talking me up to him…” His laugh was soft, uncomfortable. “Funny thing is, you’re one of my few friends he actually likes. He’s written me about it. Says you’re disciplined, ambitious. Everything I should be.” He rubbed the back of his neck, sheepishly. “Part of me wants to let you tell him whatever would make him proud. But the rest of me, I don’t want him seeing me through borrowed glory anymore.”

Redd’s teasing landed next, and Sol blinked down at the sketchbook again. The vest. The cuffs. The hair. Something in him shifted, as if he was seeing it from a new perspective.

“Half a wardrobe before breakfast,” he echoed, the words slipping out with a crooked grin. “Maybe that’s the point.”

He pushed back his chair suddenly, leaving his chai to sweat faint rings onto the table. “BRB!”

When he returned a few minutes later, his arms were stacked with journals: worn leather spines, ribbon-marked pages, sketches slipping loose from between covers. He dropped them onto the table with a satisfying thud; the top book flopped open to reveal a long, sweeping coat, detailed down to the embroidery thread.

He brushed a hand over the stack like they were treasure. “I’ve been sketching since I could hold a pencil. For me. For fun. For…” His voice faltered, a breath caught between nerves and pride. Then his grin came back, crooked but certain. “Maybe for Sponsorship Day.”

He flipped one of the journals open, revealing the first design.

The page showed a sleek dueling ensemble: a high-collared, deep navy jacket split at the hip for movement, silver piping along the seams, and hidden reinforcement stitched into the shoulders and sleeves. The trousers were slim but flexible, paired with tall boots reinforced with steel caps. Across the chest, a sash threaded with gold and silver shimmered faintly, meant to catch the light as he moved. In the margins: *functional, but sharp; makes him look like he’s already won.*

Sol rubbed the back of his neck, glancing at Caspian. “You’re all about strength and form, but I thought, why not style too? A duelist should look untouchable before the fight even starts, right?”

He rummaged through the stack of journals before settling on a paired set of designs.

The first sketch depicted a regal vest of rich crimson velvet, embroidered with threads of gold, paired with a sleeveless shirt cut to accentuate the wearer’s frame without highlighting the absence of an arm as a flaw. Instead, it appeared purposeful, the asymmetry balancing the silhouette. Slim trousers tapered into high boots, elegant yet commanding, while the hair was styled into a high bun bound with a slender metal clasp that caught the light like a crown.

On the opposite page, the design shifted into something flowing: a dress of midnight silk layered with translucent overlays, the fabric drawn to sweep like water in motion. The embroidery along the bodice shimmered in faint threads of gold, echoing the vest’s patterns without mirroring them outright. Pale crystal-beaded accents trailed from the shoulders and hem, designed to catch and refract light so that every movement would scatter faint glimmers. The hairstyle in the margin sketch was pulled into an elegant twist, jeweled pins holding it in place so that the whole look gleamed with poise.

Sol hesitated, then smiled faintly, cheeks coloring as he brushed his pencil across the page. “Yours had to be complementary,” he said at last. “You and Zašia, different strengths, but together you balance. Regal, disciplined, striking. It felt wrong to design for one of you without the other.”

Finally, Sol flipped open a journal more chaotically arranged than the rest, loose sketches spilling between the pages. He paused on one that stood out in deliberate contrast to the earlier, regal designs.

The drawing showed a cropped, tailored jacket in earthy green and black leather, its hems uneven and sleeves cuffed high to bare the forearms. Beneath it, a loose shirt was sketched to hang casually, tied at the waist or left open depending on the wearer’s mood. The trousers were sturdy but cut with ease of movement in mind, and along the seams a pattern in silver thread resembled paw prints caught mid-stride. The shoes were scuffed in the design, drawn with imperfections that looked intentional, as though they’d already seen a dozen adventures. Even the hair in the sketch was left unruly, tousled, and windswept, the kind of style that seemed effortless but matched the outfit’s roguish charm.

Sol grinned. “Yours was easy, Redd. Casual, a little roguish, something you could cook in or climb trees in, and still look like trouble while you’re doing it.”

“I can’t duel like Caspian, or bend politics like Vornakir, or turn a kitchen into a stage like Redd. But this?” His palm pressed flat over the journals. “This I can do!”

Vornakir flipped through the journal a little, mostly just wanting to admire the little sketch that was shown. But his brow quirked at his friend and then creased to meet in the center as he spoke. “Of course, if you don’t wish to utilize these, that’s quite alright. Just a thought.” He wondered aloud, giving a lofty dismissive wave of his hand to instruct the mage hand to carry away his notes. But as Sol darted away and returned, he laid his stained glass golden eyes on the new contents that were displayed. Regardless as he was laying his things out, he continued on in his Drow drawl. “If you would like me to pull rank and put him in his place, give the word and I can also do that. Nothing quite as entertaining as seeing a younger man of higher status tear into an elder.” He remarked but gave a gesture of his hand to allow Sol to speak as he slid the journal back. He seemed fond of the doodle. It made him smile, one that touched the corner of one eye.

As he displayed his art, his passion and all his emotion both on the page and spoken word it brought Vornakir’s attention rapt to the bard. He leaned forward, resting a chin on his hand with that genuine smile curving into a smirk that played at all the things he never boasted about of himself, particularly his looks. He didn’t dare interrupt the passionate display, and held his piece until after Sol finished with pure delight in his eyes. 

“Then…do it, Sol.” He spoke plainly but the resplendent shimmer of his eyes shone as bright as the man he spoke to. “If you would allow me, it would be an honor — No, pardon me. A *privilege* to be allowed to wear something as striking and commanding as one of your designs in the dueling field; I have been on the fence about returning to duel, but…” He asked, pushing his chair back to stand at his full height, straight spine and chin high but it came to lower in respect. “Before I punched above my weight class, I was undefeated in dueling people of a higher rank than me. What confidence I used to have in the field was lost with my arm, and everything I’ve worn since makes me feel like all I’ve done is remind myself of my weakness. To wear something as perfect as something you’ve drawn up? It would make me feel stronger than I have in what feels like months. And if I have to work until my knees give out and my magic evaporates, I will ensure I get strong enough to win if only to make your work proud.” 

He walked then, around the table so he could get a better look at it. This was, admittedly, a different side of him than his dorm mates had seen. Prideful enough to know when to boast his own ability, humble enough to know when to lift another up, and kind enough to want to encourage someone else to succeed — even if he would merely be the vessel instead of the contents for once.

“I of course can’t speak for the others. But if I can do anything for you, I would like to help. That goes for all of you. And if you don’t wish for me to wear it, I won’t press of course. But you should know my body is a temple most divine, and if I would allow myself to be draped in anything, it would be in the passion of a true artist.” He said, turning that easy, charming prince of a grin onto Sol. “And if this is anything like the paintings you promised to show me, I think I’ve finally been shamed by someone truly better than me.”

Sol froze at first, blinking at Vornakir as though he hadn’t quite heard him right. Then the realization sank in, and a grin broke across his face: wide, unguarded, and brighter than he usually allowed himself to be.

“You’d actually wear it?” His voice was softer, but the spark in it flared quickly. “Gods, that would mean everything. To design something that doesn’t just *fit*, but amplifies what you already carry: your discipline, your presence, the way the room shifts when you walk into it. Yes. Absolutely yes.”

He pushed up from his chair, pacing as words tumbled out faster than he could catch them. “I can already see it: cuts that sharpen your frame, fabrics that flow with you, details that demand attention.” His hands skimmed over the sketchbooks in restless arcs, like he was already shaping the pieces in the air.

“I’ve spent years sketching in secret, convincing myself it was just a distraction. But this? This could be it.” His steadied with conviction. “My spark. Sponsorship Day won’t know what hit it.”

When at last he dropped back into his seat, cheeks still flushed, his hand tapped lightly against the journals as though to anchor himself. “So let’s do it. If you’ll let me, I’ll make you something that commands the room like a crown. And the rest of you, if you’ll allow it, I want you all to wear designs that shout out who you truly are. On Sponsorship Day.”

When his mood was bright, he was as the sun despite the gray tone of his flesh. He was warm, and glowing brilliantly; the aura around him brimmed with the sort of exhilarating sensation one only got when they stepped out into a warm summer day after weeks of winter — perhaps that was the secret of *his* charisma. This was the Obsidian Prince one could see people fawning to follow, the Sun they needed in such a dark place. It was a stark contrast to the way he had been prior. Strict and unflinching, cold and distrusting, politically aware and distant. To see him now, making breakfast for people he wouldn’t even shake the hand of weeks before. Offering to let one dress him to show it off to the world, mirroring excitement and passion. 

He gestured toward himself with his only hand, twisting his face into a jesting playful smolder. “Are you kidding me? Of *course* I’d wear it. The Pale Lady as my witness, I’d drape all my Court in your finery if it’s all this tasteful. Somehow you’ve managed to create a blend of regal intrigue, nobility and cohesion. But perhaps it’s the artist in me that begs to see a court weaved in unity as they swear loyalty.” He lifted his shoulder in a dramatic shrug before he took a few steps backward and made his way back to his place at the table.

As he lowered himself down and settled in comfortably again, he glanced about to their companions. His thoughts sank into his minds palace as he imagined all of them cutting such a striking figure as they displayed Sol’s work. Was this the feeling that Zašia had tried to impart on him long ago? That he ought to see those he used to consider beneath him as important in their own ways? To find value in all those he may lead, so that he could be not one who rules by fear but by compassion and loyalty above that? The thought made him rumble a light mirthful laugh to himself. His attention was brought to Sol again, “Once we settle our affair this evening with Aurum, and you’ve had a proper rest I’ll invite you to my room. You can take my measurements and make and real time adjustments necessary — admittedly, I haven’t mentioned this to anyone yet but. Aside from the change to my eyes, my body has broken out a new layer of thicker, more encompassing scales. It will definitely require a more precise custom tailor to fit me.”

While Vornakir and Sol’s plans started to build Caspian snagged the journal containing the dueling outfit he’d been recommended. He flipped through the pages stopping on each one thoughtfully. A couple he paused and nodded appreciatively. 

“These are great Sunni. I’m not a fashion expert but you clearly are. Though you might want to pass on me as a model.  With a Saintillian contingent in residence there’s going to be a lot of hostility. I don’t want that to distract from your designs.” 

He slid the journal back to Sunni. He knocked once on the cover to show his enthusiasm for its contents.

“That goes for all of you really. When my home is involved it’ll be easier for you all to keep distant. I really don’t want anyone getting labeled a collaborator or sympathizer. I won’t hold it against anyone if they take a couple shots at me for free too.”

Sol’s grin only widened as Vornakir smoldered at him, his laugh bubbling up bright and irrepressible. “Careful, Prince, or I’ll actually take you up on that. A whole court in coordinated finery? Don’t tempt me unless you’re ready for me to start sketching banners and designing color palettes.” His tone carried a playful edge, but there was a spark in his eyes that said he’d already begun imagining it: gold-threaded uniforms, hair slicked back to resemble discipline, every piece a reflection of Vornakir’s charisma.

When the prince mentioned his scales, Sol leaned forward, interest lighting his face like sunrise. “Good. That makes it even better. Clothes aren’t meant to fight against you; they’re meant to celebrate what’s already there. If your body’s changing, then your outfit changes with it. We’ll make the scales part of the design; turn what most tailors would call a challenge into the centerpiece.” His pencil was already twitching between his fingers, eager to catch ideas before they fled.

Then Caspian spoke, and Sol’s expression softened into something quieter. He listened without interrupting, gaze lingering on his friend’s hands where they rested on the journal cover. When Caspian slid it back, Sol caught it, fingers brushing the leather, but he didn’t close it yet. Instead, he leaned an elbow on the table and tilted his head, a smile curling at one corner.

“Caspian.” His voice was gentler now, touched with earnest stubbornness. “Fashion transcends politics. Transcends courts. It doesn’t care about Saintillian this or that. It’s about how you carry yourself. About making someone look in the mirror and say, ‘that’s me, exactly as I want to be seen.’”

He hesitated, then leaned closer, his eyes catching Caspian’s with a mischievous glint. “But if it gives you peace of mind, fine. No one has to know who designed it. I’ll be your secret tailor, your nameless ghost with a sketchbook.” He winked and his grin brightened, boyish and relentless. “But let me do this for you, Cas. Please.”

And with that, he clasped his hands in mock prayer, eyes wide and shimmering like he’d practiced the perfect “puppy-dog” look. It was ridiculous, over-the-top, and utterly sincere. “Look, I can duel and paint and charm all day long, but don’t make me break out the full pout. I really, really want to design this for you.”

Redd tipped back the last of his chai, savoring the spice before setting the mug down with a soft clink. His eyes caught the sketchbook again, and this time his grin spread wide, almost boyish in its energy. “Oh, this is going to be incredible. Gorgeous. You’ve nailed me too well already—roguish, dangerous, impossible to ignore. Gods, Sol, if you actually put that into fabric, I don’t know whether the courts will crown me or lock me up. Either way, I’ll look amazing doing it.” He laughed, leaning back, then jabbed a finger upward. “And Whimsy. Don’t forget Whimsy. He’ll need his own set or he’ll never forgive us—something dramatic.”

From the rafters came a flat, unimpressed “Hoot.” Whimsy blinked once, slow and deliberate, then swiveled his head a full half-turn away from the table as if to say he wanted no part in whatever nonsense his people were plotting.

Then his grin hooked toward Caspian, sharper but no less bright. “And you. Stop talking like it’s a bad idea. Sol’s practically handing us the chance to walk into Sponsorship Day looking like legends. If the worst anyone can throw at me is being labeled a Saintillian sympathizer for standing next to you? Please. I’ve done worse than *stand* next to a Saintillan if you know what I mean—and not once lost a night’s sleep over it. My reputation’s survived scandals these people couldn’t even imagine. So don’t think for a second I, Vornakir, or any one of us will even flinch at this.”

Redd leaned forward again, elbows braced on the table, smirk tugging sharp as he gestured at Caspian. “So don’t you dare say no. Because I, for one, want to see what kind of brilliance he drapes us in.”

With outstretched arm, Vornakir raised both brows at Sol. "Oh please, I don't tempt without giving the payoff eventually; The current regime is fine, mother has a far better taste than those I've seen in our historic tomes. But to see my court united in the luxury you could provide would make for *my* regime earning its own tome to itself. So don't *you* tempt me, else I may be sewing a new sigil from a banner into my cloaks before long." He offered, with very little jest in his words. 

He then lowered his hand to rub it across his chest, down his abdomen and toward his waist on his left side. He felt the scales through the thin fabric of his shirt. There were far, far more than he used to have; Where they previously ended just shy of his ribs, now they encroached toward his navel and covered the top third of his chest below his collarbone. It worried him but the encroachment of scales had seemed to halted for now. He kept on his mask, glancing between them all and Caspian. 

"Of all people, Caspian, I would have thought you would understand this one truth," He began, with resolution and discipline firm in his tone. "It's something far too many people on this campus seem to fail grasping. And perhaps the other two here need to hear it as well, especially when it comes from someone like me." He continued, taking his water to sup from the glass and wet his lips, dramatic as ever whilst he held their attention. He sat the glass down and pointed at each one after the other. *"You are not your surname. And you are not your parents' sins."*

Caspian smiled, they sure knew how to bolster a man’s spirit. He stood up and took a little more food, Zašia wouldn’t mind right?

“Guys I appreciate the reassurance. I really do. Sunni I’ll take you up on that second offer, you’ll help me send a message to my parents with your fashion but I don’t want to paint a target on your back. I really want to wear something that’ll piss them off.”

He savored the extra food and washed it down with more of his chai as well. That helped his mood a bit more too. 

“And you two, if you’re both insistent on associating with this Saintillian then I need you to do something for me too. Gather information, there’s something about all of this we don’t understand, why send me here of all places? Why do they want Headwatch? Can I trust you to find some answers?”

He leaned forward, body coursing with a bit of excitement at the prospect of foiling his parents schemes.

Sol practically lit up under their words, his grin breaking wider and broader until it threatened to split his face in two. When Caspian agreed, he didn’t even hesitate; Sol was up from his chair in a heartbeat and pulling Caspian into a sudden, tight hug. “Yes! That’s all I needed. And don’t you worry, if it’s pissed off parents you want, I can serve that in spades. You’ll look so untouchable they’ll choke on their own outrage.”

He pulled back just enough to grin at him, then gave his shoulder a quick squeeze before stepping away, energy thrumming through him like he couldn’t stay still another second.

He wagged a finger at Redd as he grabbed his journals. “And yes, Whimsy will get something. A dramatic little harness, maybe something jeweled. Can’t have him feeling left out while his keeper steals all the glory.” He tilted his head toward the rafters with a smirk. “Sorry, Whimsy, but you’re getting couture whether you like it or not.”

He made it all the way to the doorway before pausing and glancing down at himself: messy bun, glasses, flannel pants, and the soft black tank he’d slept in. A groan escaped him. “Gods, I’m about to storm campus in my pajamas.”

With a snap of his fingers, glowing lines of gold traced across his body, shimmering threads weaving into a new outfit around him: dark, fitted trousers, a crisp shirt half-unbuttoned, and a long navy jacket draped just right for dramatic flair. The messy bun he kept, though, it felt right.

He glanced back over his shoulder with a crooked grin, journals hugged tight to his chest. “Don’t wait up, I need to capitalize on this spark of inspiration before it dies. I’ll see you all later!”

And with that, he swept out into the hall, boots tapping quickly against the floor.
+5 Development Points for all parties involved
